root.title: Social Emotional Learning(SEL) - as a Subsystem ofLearning KS [Laurence]

children.title: KIST: KS related Knowledge(concepts models), Insights(methodology & methods), Skills(techniques) & Tools - (How)
4 elements  (SAFE)
children.note:
• Sequenced: Activity coordination is designed to foster skill development.
  • Active: Active learning activities are meant to help children build and master new skills.
  • Focused: SEL focuses on developing social and personal skills.
  • Explicit: SEL is used to target specific emotional and social skills.

children.title: 15 important skills learned in SELembedded education curriculum

Emotional recognition: Emotional recognition helps learners identify and manage difficult emotions suchas jealousy and frustration. When students know how to control overwhelming emotions, they can getthrough tough situations, make sensible choices, maintain relationships and enjoy greater well-being.

Self-regulation: Students learn to stay calm through self-soothing and gain an awareness oftheir physical and mental states so they can apply calming techniques and resist impulsivebehaviors. Self-regulation is a vital skill for clearing one's mind and handling changes.
Behaviors: SEL teaches students how to replace negative communication habits and behaviorswith positive ones. Behavior skills help students bond with others and form healthy relationships.

Play skills: Students learn a variety of play skills, such as how to ask others toplay, how to share and how to be a good sport. Play skills keep theatmosphere fun and encourage children to participate and make friends.

School rules: School rules skills help children feel comfortable with classroom routines.Students also learn strategies for following directions and why it's important. Theseskills help create a learning-friendly environment and reduce disruptive behavior.

Perspective-taking: Through SEL, students build empathy skills and learn how tounderstand other viewpoints. When children can understand how others feel, theycan react to problems and misunderstandings with compassion and respect.

Basic conversation skills: SEL teaches children how to start and end conversationssmoothly. They learn when to stop talking and listen to the other person.Conversation skills help children connect with others.

Conversation topics: Students learn how to maintain topics in conversation by asking questionsand commenting. They also learn how to consider their conversation partner and tell when it's timeto change the topic. Topic skills teach children to think before they speak.

Advanced conversation skills: Advanced conversation skills teach students how to tell anarrative effectively. They also learn how to understand nonliteral language and detect humorand sarcasm. Advanced conversation skills help eliminate misunderstandings and conflict.

Nonverbal communication: Nonverbal communication skills teach children the importance of personalspace and active listening. Students also learn the value of making eye contact and understanding toneof voice and body language. These skills help students feel comfortable and connected.

Situational awareness: Students learn to consider the needs and feelings of others in different situations. Thisincludes learning how to read a room and how to be polite when sharing meals with others. Situationalawareness skills enhance shared experiences and strengthen relationships.

Problem-solving: Students learn the importance of compromising and how to use cognitiveskills to analyze a problem and determine a logical solution. Problem-solving skills helpchildren overcome challenges in school, relationships and life.

Friendship: SEL lessons teach children how to seek friends and build relationships over time. Theylearn how to give and receive compliments, handle conflict and make fair decisions. Children can usefriendship skills throughout their lives to form and maintain rewarding relationships.

Self-esteem: Students learn how to break negative thinking cycles and set positive goals. They alsolearn how to handle rejection in a healthy way and how to respond to various forms of teasing andbullying. Children need self-esteem to get through difficult situations and reach their goals.
Hygiene: SEL teaches children general hygiene practices and good manners. Students learn theimportance of improving hygiene and how personal appearance makes an impact.
Evaluating the impact of social emotional learning competencieson student success across all age and development levels.
Creating an action research project based on SEL research, theory, andpractice that will create inclusive environments for students as a whole.

Distinguishing the intersectionality between social emotional learning,equity, and cultural responsiveness in educational communities.
Reflecting critically about professional beliefs and practices inthe application and implementation of educating.
Learning the principles required not only to inspireothers, but also being an inspired educator.
Creating and maintaining effective learning environments for students.
Making subject matter cohesive and understandable forstudents of varying development levels.

Applying a growth mindset with SEL
children.note:
Using a growth mindset with Social & Emotional Learning (SEL)
Academics to take note of in this article/research mentioned: Dr. Desiree Margo, Dr. Kendra Coates

  • The Growth Mindset can be used as the foundation of improving individuals' Social and Emotional learning/academic learning
  • This utilizes the Growth Mindset which views "all forms of intelligence abilities, skills, and talents" as "malleable"
  • Research on a school environment has yielded positive results
  • The best results seem to be present in elementary students, and the principles of SEL and the principles of the Growth Mindset needs to be a part of both classroom and other school activities
  • This likely means that in the case of adults, the basic principles should also be applied to a wide range of activities, if not, preferably all aspects of life
  • Results of the research show overall improvement in students' expressiveness and use of emotion as positive impetus, improvement in staff (adults) communication skills, compassion and empathy for the people around them.
The overall school culture has also become increasingly positive.

Some of the more relatedtheories & research asfoundation of SEL
One of his key studies
children.note:
Scenario:
Heinz’s wife was dying from a particular type of cancer. Doctors said a new drug might save her. The drug had been discovered by a local chemist, and the Heinz tried desperately to buy some, but the chemist was charging ten times the money it cost to make the drug, and this was much more than the Heinz could afford.

Heinz could only raise half the money, even after help from family and friends. He explained to the chemist that his wife was dying and asked if he could have the drug cheaper or pay the rest of the money later.

The chemist refused, saying that he had discovered the drug and was going to make money from it. The husband was desperate to save his wife, so later that night he broke into the chemist’s and stole the drug.

Questions asked:
1. Should Heinz have stolen the drug?
2. Would it change anything if Heinz did not love his wife?
3. What if the person dying was a stranger, would it make any difference?
4. Should the police arrest the chemist for murder if the woman died?

Research design & results:
By studying the answers from children of different ages to these questions, Kohlberg hoped to discover how moral reasoning changed as people grew older. The sample comprised 72 Chicago boys aged 10–16 years, 58 of whom were followed up at three-yearly intervals for 20 years (Kohlberg, 1984).

Each boy was given a 2-hour interview based on the ten dilemmas. What Kohlberg was mainly interested in was not whether the boys judged the action right or wrong, but the reasons given for the decision. He found that these reasons tended to change as the children got older.

Kohlberg identified three distinct levels of moral reasoning: preconventional, conventional, and postconventional. Each level has two sub-stages.

• Stage 1. Obedience and Punishment Orientation. The child/individual is good in order to avoid being punished. If a person is punished, they must have done wrong.
• Stage 2. Individualism and Exchange. At this stage, children recognize that there is not just one right view that is handed down by the authorities. Different individuals have different viewpoints.
• Stage 3. Good Interpersonal Relationships. The child/individual is good in order to be seen as being a good person by others. Therefore, answers relate to the approval of others.
• Stage 4. Maintaining the Social Order. The child/individual becomes aware of the wider rules of society, so judgments concern obeying the rules in order to uphold the law and to avoid guilt.
• Stage 5. Social Contract and Individual Rights. The child/individual becomes aware that while rules/laws might exist for the good of the greatest number, there are times when they will work against the interest of particular individuals. 
The issues are not always clear-cut. For example, in Heinz’s dilemma, the protection of life is more important than breaking the law against stealing.
• Stage 6. Universal Principles. People at this stage have developed their own set of moral guidelines which may or may not fit the law. The principles apply to everyone.
E.g., human rights, justice, and equality. The person will be prepared to act to defend these principles even if it means going against the rest of society in the process and having to pay the consequences of disapproval and or imprisonment. Kohlberg doubted few people reached this stage.

children.title: Basic summary of stages and outcomes
Overview of theory
children.note:
• Erikson was a developmental psychologist who specialized in child psychoanalysis who developed the theory of psychosocial development.
  • His theory assumes that human development is a product of three factors: biological maturity, interaction between individuals and social groups and intrinsic contributors from individuals.
  • The psychosocial theory refers to how a person's individual needs (personal) mesh with the needs or demands of society (social).
  • According to him, a person passes through eight developmental stages that build on each other.
  • At each stage we face a crisis/conflict that stems from social interactions and relationships (familial or others). By resolving the crisis/conflict, we develop psychological strengths or character traits that help us become confident and healthy people.
  • Conversely, if these conflicts are not successfully dealt with, they may not develop the essential skills required for a strong sense of self (identity)
  • Each stage of Erikson's theory builds on the preceding stages for the next period of development. Each conflict that is experienced would become a turning point in an individual's development

children.title: Criticism/limitations
children.note:
• The mechanisms for resolving conflicts and proceeding to other stages of development have vague descriptions and are not developed.
  • As such, the theory does not detail the "proper type of experience" necessary at each stage for successful resolutions for the conflicts that arise to create an optimal result.
  • The way culture encourages or inhibits development are not clearly elaborated

children.title: Relevancy
children.note:
• The theory provides a broad framework from which to view development throughout the entire lifespan.
  • It allows us to emphasize the social nature of human beings and the important influence that social relationships have on development.
  • Researchers have found evidence supporting Erikson's ideas about identity and have further identified different sub-stages of identity formation. (link)
  • Some research suggests that people who form strong personal identities during adolescence are better capable of forming intimate relationships during early adulthood.
  • Other research suggests, however, that identity formation and development continues well into adulthood. (link)

children.title: Bandura - Social Cognitive Theory/SocialLearning Theory
SCT Model (Bandura, 2000)
children.note:
EBOOK: Predicting and Changing Health Behaviour: Research and Practice with Social Cognition Models (Chapter 7, p. 225-226)
  • According to social cognitive theory, human motivation and action are extensively regulated by forethought.
  • This anticipatory control mechanism involves expectations that might refer to outcomes of undertaking a specific action. The theory outlines a number of crucial factors that influence behavior.
  • This first factor is perceived self-efficacy, which is concerned with people's beliefs in their capabilities to perform a specific action required to attain a desired outcome.
  • Outcome expectancies are the other core construct of SCT, which are concerned with people's beliefs about the possible conqsequences of their actions.
  • In addition to these two cognitions, SCT also includes goals, perceived impediments and facilitators.
  • These constructs are displayed in the figure 7.1, which illustrates their interplay throughout the behavior change process.

Self-efficacy
  • Perceived self-efficacy is concerned with individuals’ beliefs in their capability to exercise control over challenging demands and their own functioning (Bandura 1997).
  • In a unifying theory of behavior change, Bandura hypothesized that expectations of self-efficacy are self-regulatory cognitions that determine whether instrumental actions will be initiated, how much effort will be expended, and how long it will be sustained in the face of obstacles and failures.
  • Self-efficacy is considered as an influence on action because it contributes to motivation or the lack thereof.
  • Perceived  self-efficacy  represents  the  confidence  that  one  can employ the skills necessary to resist temptation, cope with stress, and mobilize one's resources required  to  meet  the  situational  demands.
  • It's contribution to motivation includes bolstering (from whatever contributing source) persistence in the face of barriers and setbacks and to affecting the amount of effort being put in (Bandura, 1997).

There are mainly four sources of self-efficacy:
  1. Self-efficacy levels can be enhanced through personal accomplishment or mastery as far as success is attributed internally and can be repeated.
  2. When a 'model person' – that is, someone similar to the individual – successfully masters a difficult situation, social comparison processes can enhance self-efficacy beliefs.
  3. verbal persuasion by others can also enhance self-efficacy beliefs (e.g. a health educator reassures a patient that she will definitely perform cancer screening properly due to her competence).
  4. The last source of influence is emotional arousal – that is, the person may experience no apprehension in a threatening situation, and as a result may feel capable of mastering the situation.

children.title: Borrowed model from Lent et al. (1994)
Model of how basic career interests develop overtime (with a liberal interpretation and applicationof Bandura's SCT) (Lent et al., 1994)
Overview
children.note:
• Started as Social Learning Theory but was developed into Social Cognitive Theory by Bandura in 1986.
  • He posits that learning occurs in a social context with a dynamic and reciprocal interaction of the person, environment, and behavior.
  • Social cognitive theory places focuses on social influence and its emphasis on external and internal social reinforcement.
  • The theory takes into account a person's past experiences, which factor into whether behavioral action will occur.
  • These past experiences influences reinforcements, expectations, and expectancies, all of which shape whether a person will engage in a specific behavior and the reasons why a person engages in that behavior.

Social Cognitive Theory basics:
  • Learning can be a result of watching others' actions and the outcomes of the action (positive /negative); known as observational learning or modelling
  • Most human behaviors are learned through observation of models (which Bandura calls all individuals external of the self)
  • New behaviors are coded as information and serves as a guide for action in the future.
  • An individual's mental state and motivation play a role in determining whether a behavior is learned or not.
  • This is described as intrinsic reinforcement: a form of internal rewards, such as pride, satisfaction, and sense of accomplishment.

children.title: Key factors for observational learning
children.note:
• Attention
Your focus on a model/point of observation. Anything that distracts your attention
is going to have a negative effect on observational learning. If the model is interesting or if a situation is unique, you are far more likely to dedicate your full attention to learning.

  • Retention
The ability to store information. Retention is an important part of the learning process and can be affected by a number of factors, but the ability to pull up information later and act on it is vital to observational learning.
 
 
  • Reproduction
Enacting the behavior observed. Once you have paid attention to the model and retained the information, the behavior should be reproduced. Further practice of the learned behavior leads to improvement and skill advancement.
 
 
  • Motivation
Reason for reproduction/learning. In order for observational learning to be successful, you need to be motivated to imitate the behavior that has been modelled. Motivation can include reinforcement and punishment. For example, if you see another student rewarded with extra credit for being to class on time, you might start to show up a few minutes early each day.

children.title: Relation to SEL
children.note:
• Bandura's social cognitive theory indicates that learning occurs in a social context with
  • complex and mutual interaction of the person, their behavior, and the environment
  • The theory utilizes past experiences as a factor into whether a behavioral action will occur.
  • SCT stresses the importance of a continuous interaction among one’s behavior, one’s personal factors, and the environment.
  • SCT also accounts for past experiences and their impact on whether a behavioral action will occur; therefore, social cognitive theory was the basis of incorporating social emotional learning skills in the classroom (Bandura, 1989; 1997).
  • Bandura’s theory illustrates that students with greater self-efficacy are more confident in their abilities to be successful and that greater self-efficacy is a product of a student’s experiences, observations, and influences from the environment (Bandura, 1989).
  • Bandura (1997) states that teaching students to be able to educate themselves by providing them with self-regulatory efficacy skills (e.g., skills for planning, organizing, adapting one’s own motivation, etc.) is an essential goal of the education system.
  • Bandura (1997) states that a student’s robust sense of self-efficacy creates a high level of motivation and academic interest and success, while a low self-efficacy reveals negative disincentives.
  • Students with high self-efficacy are able to attempt tasks and persevere even when the tasks are overly difficult, while students with low self-efficacy tend to give up easily (Tollefson, 2000).
  • Students who have a high self-efficacy expectation (e.g., belief that they can succeed) and a high outcome expectation (e.g., belief that specific actions lead to specific outcomes) approach academics with self-confidence and proceed through the challenging task with optimism because they believe they can succeed with the abilities that they possess (Tollefson, 2000).
  • In SCT, there are three ways a person can change their basic behavior process:
  1. new behavior patterns are formed
  2. how behaviors change under different situations
  3. how behaviors are maintained

  • Self-efficacy also includes the belief that students can change their behavior, if they are motivated to succeed, and once successful how to maintain the new changes (Bandura, 1997)
  • When students lack self-efficacy, they lack the motivation to change behavior in difficult situations which leads students to choose unhealthy activities, which leads to unsuccessful students (Bandura, 1997)
  • Bandura (1997) suggested that students are able to successfully get through challenging situations because of their high self-regulatory efficacy that uses behavioral and cognitive strategies (e.g. belief in self, coping skills, avoiding risky situations, etc.). A high self-regulatory allows students to enter an environment and make positive decisions (Bandura, 1997).
  • Students who are successful in and out of school are able to self-regulate their motivation by using behavioral and cognitive strategies (Bandura, 1997).
  • Self-regulatory skills allow students to monitor their thoughts and behaviors in difficult situations to be able to modify their responses so they can succeed (Bandura, 1997). The more self-regulatory skills students acquire, the more positive self-efficacy which will then result in students who are motivated to succeed and meet their goals (Bandura, 1997).
  • These self-regulatory skills can be related to SEL's major five categories of focus, such as self-awareness and self-management, which have shown to contribute positively to academic performance, behaviors and perspectives, etc.

children.title: Criticism/limitations
children.note:
• The theory assumes that changes in the environment will automatically lead to changes in the person, when this may not always be true
  • The theory is loosely organized, based solely on the dynamic interplay between person, behavior, and environment. It is unclear the extent to which each of these factors into actual behavior and if one is more influential than another
  • The theory does not focus on emotion or motivation, other than through reference to past experience. There is minimal attention on these factors

children.title: Emotions can impact memory negatively or positively
children.note:
• Effects of emotion on learning and memory are not always univalent, as it can either enhance or impair learning and long-term memory retention depending on a range of factors.
  • Emotional inﬂuences should be carefully considered in educational courses design to maximize learner engagement as well as improve learning and long-term retention of the material (Shen et al., 2009). This is especially the case in fast-evolving educational approaches brought forth by technology, such as intelligent tutoring systems (ITSs) and massive open online courses (MOOCs).
  • There are numerous studies that have shown that emotion has influence on our cognitive processes (Vuilleumier, 2005), learning and memory (Phelps, 2004; Um et al. 2012), reasoning (Jung et al., 2014) and problem-solving (Isen et al., 1987).
  • There are some studies suggesting the possibility that emotional stimuli appear to consume more attentional resources than non-emotional stimuli (Schupp et al., 2007).
  • Moreover, attentional and motivational components of emotion have been linked to heightened learning and memory (Pekrun, 1992; Seli et al., 2016). Hence, emotional experiences/stimuli appear to be remembered vividly and accurately, with great resilience over time.
  • Panksepp (1998) personality dimensions: the seven primary emotional systems/prototype emotional states - SEEKING, RAGE, FEAR, LUST, CARE,
PANIC/GRIEF, AND PLAY that represent basic foundations for living and
  • learning

children.title: Negative stimuli can enhance memory retention
Stress
children.note:
• Stress has been reported to facilitate and/or impair both learning and memory, depending on intensity and duation (Vogel and Schwabe, 2016).
  • More speciﬁcally, mild and acute stress facilitates learning and cognitive performance, while excess and chronic stress impairs learning and is detrimental to memory performance.
  • Consequently, understanding, managing, and regulating emotion is critical to the development of enhanced learning programs informed by the signiﬁcant impacts of learning and memory under diﬀerent types of stress (Vogel and Schwabe, 2016).

children.title: Negative events
children.note:
• A recent study reported that negative learning-centered state (confusion) improve learning because of an increased focus of attention on learning material that leads to higher performances on post tests and transfer tests (D’Mello et al., 2014)
  • Similarly, emotionally enhanced memory functions have been reported in relation to selective attention elicited by emotionally salient stimuli (Vuilleumier, 2005; Schupp et al., 2007).
  • Both emotion and motivation are crucial for the maintenance of psychological and physiological homeostasis, while emotional roles are particularly important in process of encoding new information containing emotional components
  • The latter increases attention toward salient new information by selectively enhancing detection, evaluation, and extraction of data for memorization.

children.title: Motivational components can also promotelearning and memory retrieval
children.note: • Motivational components promote learning and enhance subsequent memory retrieval while generalizing new events consequent to adaptive physiological changes.

children.title: Hierarchies of circular emotional controland cognitive regulation
Classification of memory systems in thebrain
Motivation and meaning as psychologicalfactors
children.note:
• To go in tandem with "objectives" and "meaning" under "what drives you to learn" and "why learn?"
  • Relates back to Bandura's self-efficacy model, which refers to "a person’s particular set of beliefs that determine how well one can execute a plan of action in prospective situations (Bandura, 1977).
  • Self-efficacy seems to be defined by people's beliefs in their capabilities to exercise control over their own functioning and over events that affect their lives and one's sense of self-efficacy is likely to provide the foundation for motivation, well-being, and personal accomplishment.
  • Self-efficacy also has a strong correlation to confidence

children.title: Bandura's Self-Efficacy model
children.note:
• Self-efficacy theory emphasizes the importance of the individual and the individual's perceptions of his/her personal capabilities as key determinants of successful outcomes (Gallagher, 2012)
  • It explicitly focuses on how individuals and communities can be empowered with a sense of agency that will facilitate goal attainment
  • Our self-efficacy may strongly relate to our self-worth and meaning/value as a person
  • A person with stronger self-efficacy is likely to be more confident, willing to challenge the views presented to them and promotes accomplishment and a better personal well-being.
  • Self-efficacy also relates to higher motivation levels where experiences of success, besides a more positive, confident mindset, can contribute to improved motivation
  • People with high levels of self-efficacy are not only more likely to succeed, but they are also more likely to bounce back and recover from failure. They are more resilient.
  • Bandura proposed that perceived self-efficacy influences what coping behavior is initiated when an individual is met with stress and challenges, along with determining how much effort will be expended to reach one’s goals and for how long those goals will be pursued (1999)
  • As such, there is a direct association between self-efficacy beliefs and variables important for academic success, such as persistence and performance.

children.title: Self-efficacy Model (Bandura, 1986)
children.note:
Performance Accomplishments
Personal assessment information that is based on an individual’s accomplishments. Previous successes raise mastery expectations, while repeated failures lower them.

Vicarious Experience
Gained by observing others perform activities successfully.
This is often referred to as modeling, and it can generate expectations in observers that they can improve their performance by learning from what they have observed.

Social Persuasion
Activities where people are led, through suggestion, into believing that they can cope successfully with specific tasks. Coaching and giving evaluative feedback on performance are common types of social persuasion.

Physiological and Emotional States
The individual’s physiological or emotional states influence self-efficacy judgments concerning specific tasks. Emotional reactions to such tasks (e.g., anxiety) can lead to negative judgments of one’s ability to complete the tasks.

children.title: Other related/derivative models
children.note:
From: Relationships between academic self-efficacy, learning-related emotions, and metacognitive learning strategies with academic performance in medical students: a structural equation model (Hayat et al., 2020)

Based on components from Social Cognitive Theory, Expectancy-Value Theory and Self-Efficacy Theory.

children.title: Self-efficacy Model + Social cognitiveTheory
children.note:
• It contends that people learn through reciprocal interactions with their environment and by observing others, rather than simply through direct reinforcement of behaviours
  • It is posited that cognition governs how individuals interpret their environment and self-regulate their thoughts, feelings and actions.
  • The active process of regulating one's behaviour and manipulating the environment in pursuit of personal goals is fundamental to functioning as a motivated individual.
  • Unlike broader notions of self-concept or self-esteem, self-efficacy is domain, task and context-specific.
  • E.g. For instance, a medical student might report fairly high self-efficacy for simple suturing but may have much lower self-efficacy for other surgical procedures, or might have lower self-efficacy in a competitive environment than in a cooperative one.
  • Self-efficacy should not be confused with outcome expectation, but the two, while possibly diverging, can be positively correlated.
  • Mastery experiences provide information about one's successes, but also failures. As such, successful experiences increase self-efficacy beliefs, while experiences of failure lower them.
  • Vicarious experiences provide information about modeled attainments of others, which influence one's self-efficacy beliefs by demonstrating and transferring competencies (model learning) and by providing a point of reference for social comparison.
  • Verbal persuasion by“significant others" (Bandura, 1997; p. 101) can convince people of their capabilities, especially if this persuasion comes from a credible source.
  • Physiological and affective states provide information about physiological and affective arousal during situations in which the capability in the domain in question is demonstrated.
  • Bandura emphasizes the importance of a cognitive processing stage, at which the information from each source is interpreted and integrated, whereby different weights are assigned to the sources.
  • Among the four sources of self-efficacy, mastery experiences generally have the strongest effect on self-efficacy development, because they are the most authentic indicators of one's capabilities.

children.title: Self-Efficacy, meaning, and learning
children.note:
• Dewitz and Walsh (2009) posited a connection between "meaning of life" or the lack thereof with self-efficacy levels in college students, which resulted in an "existential vacuum".
  • They believed that self-efficacy beliefs may be connected to the development and strength of an individuals' feeling of purpose of life.

children.title: Other motivation and learning relatedmodels
children.note:
In expectancy-value theory, motivation is a function of the expectation of success and perceived value.

Attribution theory focuses on the causal attributions learners create to explain the results of an activity, and classifies these in terms of their locus, stability and controllability.

Social- cognitive theory emphasizes self-efficacy as the primary driver of motivated action, and also identifies cues that influence future self-efficacy and support self-regulated learning.

Goal orientation theory suggests that learners tend to engage in tasks with concerns about mastering the content (mastery goal, arising from a ‘growth’ mindset regarding intelligence and learning) or about doing better than others or avoiding failure (performance goals, arising from a ‘fixed’ mindset).

Finally, self-determination theory proposes that optimal performance results from actions motivated by intrinsic interests or by extrinsic values that have become integrated and internalized. Satisfying basic psychosocial needs of autonomy, competence and relatedness promotes such motivation.

Looking across all five theories, we note recurrent themes of competence, value, attributions, and interactions between individuals and the learning context.

children.title: Expectancy-Value Theory Model
children.note:
• Expectancy-value theories usually identify two key independent factors that influence behaviour:
(1) the degree to which individuals believe they will be successful if they try (expectancy of success), and
(2) the degree to which they perceive that there is a personal importance, value or intrinsic interest in doing the task (task value).
  • Expectancy of success is more than a perception of general competence and represents a future-oriented conviction that one can accomplish the anticipated task
  • E.g. If I do not believe I will be successful in accomplishing a task, I am unlikely to begin.
  • Expectancy of success is shaped by motivational beliefs that fall into three broad categories: goals, self-concept and task difficulty.
  • Goals refer to specific short- and long-term learning objectives.
  • Self-concept refers to general impressions about one's capacity in this task domain (e.g. academic ability, athletic prowess, social skills or good looks).
  • Task difficulty refers to the perceived (not necessarily actual) difficulty of the specific task.

children.title: Background
children.note:
Behaviorism: A theory of learning based on the idea that all behaviors are acquired through interaction with environmental stimuli, and that innate factors have little influence on behavior.
  • The psychological school of behaviorism came into existence in the early 19th century when psychologists wanted to be able to focus on observable, quantifiable factors so that psychological studies were scientific and reliable.
  • As such, behaviorism places an emphasis on studying external behaviors instead of introspective psychological factors.

There are several assumptions that are made with behaviorism:
  • All behavior is learned from, and shaped by the environment
  • Behaviors are learnt through classical or operant conditioning
  • The stimulus-response leads to a resulting behavior, and behaviorism posits that even complexed actions can be broken down as a reaction to a stimulus
  • Behaviorism is primarily concerned with observable behavior, as opposed to internal events like thinking and emotion.

However, other concepts in behaviorism, especially for learning, are commonly used in the classroom environment; specifically positive and negative reinforcement.

children.title: Background
children.note:
• Harlow was an American psychologist best known for his maternal-separation, dependency needs, and social isolation experiments on rhesus monkeys, which manifested the importance of caregiving and companionship to social and cognitive development.

  • His experiments with primates have helped to develop our understanding of social relationships and their impact in early childhood development.

  • Harlow and his fellow social and cognitive psychologists argued that the predominant view that babies were attached to their mothers because food was provided was lacking and overlooked other aspects that were present in a functional parent-child relationship.

  • They believed  that perspective limited and overlooked the importance of comfort, companionship, and love in promoting healthy development.

children.title: Rhesus Monkey Experiment
children.note:
• The experiment that he is known by is the Rhesus Monkey experiment. Harlow removed infant Rhesus monkey away from their mothers and raised them in various ways.

  • Some were raised socially isolated from peers (which resulted in disturbed behavior such as self-mutilation and staring blankly. When reintroduced to a group of their peers, they were unable to interact and many stayed separate from the group even after reintroduction)

  • Others were raised away from mothers and they developed social deficits and reclusive tendencies.

  • His experiment with surrogate mothers (one cloth without food, and the other, a steel cage with a

  • milk bottle) produced evidence for the parent-child attachment relationship and importance of maternal touch in childhood development.

children.title: Background
children.note:
• John B. Watson, an American psychologist, was the pioneering psychologist in developing the behaviorism school in psychology.

  • His belief was that psychology should be based on scientific observable behavior

  • He helped to set the stage for behaviorism in psychology, which came to dominate the field post WWII

  • While behaviourism may not be as relevant anymore, many of the concepts and principles are still widely used today

  • Conditioning and behaviour modification are still widely used in therapy and behavioural training to help clients change problematic behaviours and develop new skills

children.title: Little Albert experiment
children.note:
• A well-known, yet controversial, experiment in the early years of behaviorism was conducted by John B. Watson and his wife.

  • Known as the Little Albert experiment, Watson and co. conditioned an 11 month old infant to fear a white rat, and in extension, anything else that shared similar visual and physical properties (dogs, rabbits, fur coats, cotton wool).

  • These fears were a part of a larger generalization that extended from the conditioned fear of the white rat.

  • This experiment reinforced the idea of stimulus-response in the Behaviorism school of thought.

  • This experiment, in hindsight, was questionable in terms of its ethics and conclusions as the standards of the tests were not in line with modern day scientific questioning.

children.title: Others (self-improvement opportunities not based on location)
Best to begin understanding SEL duringformative years during identity formation
Workplace
children.note:
Why should workplace interventions for developing/improving emotional intelligence be implemented?
  • Workplace interventions make sense because employers have the means and motivation for providing necessary training experiences.
  • This is also sensible as most working adults spend majority of their waking hours at work than any other place. Thus, whether the motivation is to promote greater organizational productivity and competitiveness, individual success and career advancement, or physical health and personal well-being, the workplace is the optimal setting to do so.

children.title: Why
children.note:
• Decades of research and practice in social, emotional, and cognitive development have provided a foundational body of knowledge that demonstrates that
  1. social, emotional, and cognitive development are deeply intertwined in the brain and in behavior and together influence school and life outcomes including higher education, physical and mental health, economic well-being, and civic engagement
  2. social, emotional, and cognitive skills and competencies grow, and are fostered, in rich and supportive relationships and are influenced by the experiential and contextual landscape of human development
  3. there exists an array of programs and practices that have been shown to be effective in cultivating and supporting this body of competencies that can be adopted by and implemented in formal and informal learning environments from early childhood through adolescence (Durlak, Domitrovich, Weissberg & Gullotta, 2015; Jones & Kahn, 2017; Jones & Doolittle, 2017).

  • For example, research has shown that social and emotional skills are related to positive academic, social, and mental health outcomes (e.g., Moffitt et al., 2011; Greenberg, Domitrovich, Weissberg, & Durlak, 2017).
  • Correlational studies show that classrooms function more effectively and student learning increases when children can focus their attention, manage negative emotions, navigate relationships with peers and adults, and persist in the face of difficulty (e.g., Osher et al., 2016; Jones & Doolittle, 2017).
  • Children who effectively manage their thinking, attention, and behavior are also more likely to have better grades and higher standardized test scores (e.g., Jones, Bailey & Jacob, 2014).
  • Children with strong social skills are more likely to make and sustain friendships, initiate positive relationships with teachers, participate in classroom activities, and be positively engaged in learning (e.g., Denham, 2006).
  • Social and emotional skills in childhood have been tied to important life outcomes 20 to 30 years later, including having success in the labor market, graduating from high school and succeeding in college, being healthy, not using substances, and not being involved with the justice system.

children.title: SEL is important for identity development, emotionmanagement and establishing social relationships
children.note:
SEL, in a general gist, teaches us to manage ourselves more effectively and regulate the way we process information in a different way. SEL teaches us to process stress triggers throughout life differently and manage the emotions we face, as well as impulses to produce a reaction that is more positive and productive. A simple analogy would be that we are using SEL to "hijack" our body's ability to process the world meaningfully.

SEL focuses on the learner rather than the engaging party. Like learning in a classroom, SEL teaches and targets skill sets and competencies that underscore the ability to learn to create the best conditions for learning or facing different types of situations.

SEL teaches emotional management competencies, which are useful for managing stress and keeping our mental state healthy.

children.title: SEL skills are related to careerimprovement and are in demand
children.note:
Do SEL skills relate to career, business, and economic success?
  • SEL curiculum teach self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, relationship skills, and responsible decision making, all of which do relate to career and economic success in adult life.
  • The suite of competencies that SEL focuses on (represented in the CASEL core competencies framework) meet the many employability needs identified by business leaders
  • Some of these skills include: self motivation, self-discipline, time management, communication, problem solving, critical thinking, and relationship building.
  • These skills contribute to the social and emotional competencies of individuals.
  • Much of the aforementioned skills are also fall under the emotional intelligence matrix.

E.g. from reports and surveys:
  • 2012 Survey with 704 employers done by The Chronicle of Higher Education and American Public Media's Marketplace: half of those surveyed had trouble finding recent graduates to fill vacancies; applicants had the technically prowess, but lacked the communication, adaptability, decision-making and problem solving to do the job.
  • Business Roundtable/Change the Equation survey: 98% of interviewed CEOs reported they have problems "finding candidates with the competencies and training to fill open positions"
  • Report from Association for Career and Technical Education (ACTE), National Association of state Directors of Career Technical Education (CTE), Partnership for 21st Century Skills (P21) found that employers want workers with 4Cs skills: critical thinking and problem solving, creativity and innovation, communication, and collaboration
  • 2015 Forbes report on the skills employers most desire in college graduates rank these skills at the top: the ability to work as part of a team, the ability to solve problems and make decisions, and the ability to communicate verbally
  • Of the 16 skills identified in a World Economic Forum report as important for the 21st century—including foundational literacy, competency in approaching challenges, and character qualities—12 are social and emotional skills (needs to be confirmed which 12/16 skills)

children.title: SEL promotes and teaches skills & competencies that areimportant for employability
children.note:
The importance of social and emotional competence at the workplace
  • Such competencies are critical for effective performance in most jobs. 
  • Studies in dozens of organizations suggest that about two-thirds of competencies linked to superior performance are emotional or social qualities such as with others (Boyatzis, 1982; Lusch & Serpkeuci, 1990; McClelland, 1999; Rosier, 1994-1996; Spencer & Spencer, 1993)
  • In leadership positions, almost 90 percent of competencies necessary for success are social and emotional in nature (Goleman, 1998)
  • Employers have long recognized that the competencies associated with emotional intelligence are crucial: A U.S. Department of Labor national survey conducted in the mid-1980s presented a list of qualities that employers were looking for in entry level workers; it was dominated by social and emotional qualities such as adaptability, personal self-management, confidence, motivation to work towards goals, group and interpersonal effectiveness, teamwork, leadership potential, etc. (Carnevale, gainer, & Meltzer, 1988)
  • People need those qualities even more today as the workplace is increasingly characterized by competitive pressures, constant change, and downsizing in a VUCA world.
  • Recent data (included in the previous node notes) suggests similar issues as a major concern for employers.

children.title: SEL contributes to improved emotionalintelligence and performance
children.note:
CASEL's SEL framework covers the competencies that are found in the Emotional Intelligence descriptors, which are mainly divided into two categories:

Personal competence is comprised of self-awareness and self-management.
  • Self-management: Self-management is about keeping disruptive emotions and impulses in check, acting in congruence with your values, handling change flexibly, and pursuing goals and opportunities despite obstacles and setbacks.
  • Self-awareness: Self-awareness has to do with self-confidence, awareness of your emotional state, recognizing how your behavior impacts others and paying attention to how others influence your emotional state.

Social competence is comprised of social awareness and relationship management.
  • Social awareness: Social awareness competencies include empathy, to be able to pick up the
moods of others, and understanding others, connecting with them emotionally.
  • Relationship management: Relationship management competencies involve getting along well with
others, handling conflict, clearly expressing ideas and using sensitivity to manage others’ feelings.

So how does better emotional intelligence relate to success?
  • Past studies have shown that overall improved emotional intelligence contributes to better performance and success (See graph included in attachment; Freedman & Fariselli, 2016).
  • Being able to relate to others, and being aware of your own emotions, strengths and weaknesses enables individuals to react more consciously to the situation that they are presented with.
  • Good control of their emotions will allow them to make better, level-headed decisions instead of rash decisions made from emotional impulses.
  • A good EI will also help them to understand better what is it that they want to aim for, as they have a clearer understanding of their current position, both emotionally and socially with their better awareness in both areas.

children.title: For companies: increase employee retention rates, productivityand soft skills for general overall performance improvement
children.note:
Source: Irmi [Emotional Intelligence in the Workplace: Why It Matters More Than Personality]
  • A Fortune 500 company in financial services proved that its high EQ salespeople produced 18 percent more than the lower EQ salespeople produced.
  • One recent study conducted by a Dallas corporation measured that the productivity difference between its low-scoring emotional intelligence employees and its high-scoring emotional intelligence employees was 20 times.
  • A Texas-based Fortune 500 Company had utilized personality assessments for candidate selection for years with little results in reducing turnover in its high-turnover sales force. After turning to an emotional intelligence-based selection assessment and EQ training and development program, it increased retention by 67 percent in the first year, which it calculated added $32 million to its bottom line in reduced turnover costs and increased sales revenues
  • A large metropolitan hospital reduced its critical care nursing turnover from 65 percent to 15 percent within 18 months of implementing an emotional intelligence screening assessment.

children.title: Social-emotional competencies (Singapore'sMinistry of education framework)
children.note:
Source: Ministry of Education, Singapore: SEL
Social and Emotional Learning (SEL) refers to the soft skills and emotional intelligence skills that are essential for individuals, children or adults alike to help them:
  • Develop healthy identities
  • Recognize and manage their own emotions
  • Develop a sense of responsibility, care and concern for others
  • Relate to others and develop positive relationships
  • Handle challenges
  • Make responsible decisions and act for the good of self, others and the society

All the aforementioned skills and attitudes can be taught, modeled and practiced to result in positive outcomes.

Source: CASEL
Definition of SEL:
“Social and emotional learning (SEL) is the process through which all young people and adults acquire and apply the knowledge, skills and attitudes to develop healthy identities, manage emotions and achieve personal and collective goals, feel and show empathy for others, establish and maintain supportive relationships, and make responsible and caring decisions."


Other possible academic references:
  • Conley, C.S. (2015). "SEL in Higher Education." In J.A. Durlak, C.E. Domitrovich, R.P. Weissberg, & T.P. Gullotta (Eds.), Handbook of Social and Emotional Learning. New York: Guilford Press.
Sklad, M., Diekstra, R., Ritter, M.D., Ben, J., & Gravesteijn, C. (2012). "Effectiveness of school-based universal social, emotional, and behavioral programs: Do they enhance students' development in the area of skill, behavior, and adjustment?" Psychology in the Schools, 49(9), pp.892-909.

children.title: Recognize and manage their own emotions
Develop a sense of responsibility, care and concern for others
Relate to others and develop positive relationships
Make responsible decisions and act for the good of self, others and the society
Guidance for learning about these competencies
EQ Competencies that correlate toworkplace success (Irmi.com)
Intuition and empathy
children.note:
Our awareness of others' feelings, needs, and concerns. This competency is important in the workplace for the following reasons:
  • Understanding others: an intuitive sense of others' feelings and perspectives, and showing an active interest in their concerns and interests
  • Customer service orientation: the ability to anticipate, recognize, and meet customers' needs
  • People development: ability to sense what others need in order to grow, develop, and master their strengths
  • Leveraging diversity: cultivating opportunities through diverse people

children.title: Political acumen and social skills
children.note:
One's adeptness at inducing desirable responses in others. This competency is important in the workplace for the following reasons:
  • Influence: using effective tactics and techniques for persuasion and desired results
  • Communication: sending clear and convincing messages that are understood by others
  • Leadership: inspiring and guiding groups of people
  • Change catalyst: initiating and/or managing change in the workplace people
  • Building bonds: nurturing instrumental relationships for business success
  • Collaboration and cooperation: working with coworkers and business partners toward shared goals
  • Team capabilities: creating group synergy in pursuing collective goals

children.title: Self-awareness
children.note:
Knowing one's internal states, preferences, resources, and intuitions. This competency is important in the workplace for the following reasons:

  • Emotional awareness: recognizing one's emotions and their effects and impact on those around us
  • Accurate self-assessment: knowing one's strengths and limits
  • Self-confidence: being sure about one's self-worth and capabilities

children.title: Self-regulation
children.note:
This involves managing one's internal states, impulses, and resources. This competency is important in the workplace for the following reasons:

  • Self-control: managing disruptive emotions and impulses
  • Trustworthiness: maintaining standards of honesty and integrity
  • Conscientiousness: taking responsibility and being accountable for personal performance
  • Adaptability: flexibility in handling change
  • Innovation: being comfortable with an openness to novel ideas, approaches, and new information

children.title: Self-expectations and motivation
children.note:
The emotional tendencies that guide or facilitate reaching goals. This competency is important in the workplace for the following reasons:

  • Achievement drive: striving to improve or meet a standard of excellence we impose on ourselves
  • Commitment: aligning with the goals of the group or organization
  • Initiative: being ready to act on opportunities without having to be told
  • Optimism: being persistent in pursuing goals despite obstacles and setbacks
